sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The player boundary is [[33, 12], [33, 15], [35, 15], [35, 12]], which is a 2x3 rectangle (rows 33-35, cols 12-15). That's 2*3=6 pixels... but the player has 12 pixels. Wait, the boundary is the outer perimeter, not the cells. Let me think about this.
The boundary corners are [33, 12], [33, 15], [35, 15], [35, 12]. This describes a rectangle from row 33 to row 35 and col 12 to col 15. That's 3 rows (33, 34, 35) and 4 cols (12, 13, 14, 15), which is 3*4=12 pixels. OK, that matches.
So the player occupies rows 33-35 and cols 12-15. The c frame at (29.5, 17.5) occupies rows 28-30 and cols 16-19 (approximately). The player's right edge is col 15 and the c frame's left edge is col 16. So there's a 1-column gap between them. The player should be able to move RIGHT.
But the RIGHT action didn't move the player. Let me check the current state after the UP action.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After UP:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] != 'w':
        b = n['boundary']
        center = (sum(p[0] for p in b)/len(b), sum(p[1] for p in b)/len(b))
        print(f"  ({n['color']}): center={center}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={b}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the player moved! The player was at (34, 13.5) and now is at (30, 13.5). The player moved UP by 4 rows (from row 34 to row 30). That matches the expected movement of 4 rows per UP action.
But wait, the player is now at (30, 13.5) and the c frame is at (29.5, 17.5). The player occupies rows 29-31 and cols 12-15. The c frame occupies rows 28-31 and cols 16-19. The player's right edge is col 15 and the c frame's left edge is col 16. So they're adjacent horizontally but don't overlap.
But earlier, the RIGHT actions didn't move the player. Let me check - the player was at (34, 13.5) and tried to go RIGHT. The player occupies rows 33-35 and cols 12-15. The c frame at (29.5, 17.5) occupies rows 28-31 and cols 16-19. The player's top edge is row 33 and the c frame's bottom edge is row 31. So there's a 1-row gap (row 32) between them. The player should be able to move RIGHT without hitting the c frame.
Wait, but the W frame at (26, 13.5) now occupies rows 24-28 and cols 12-15. The player is at rows 29-31 and cols 12-15. So the W frame is above the player. The player moved UP from (34, 13.5) to (30, 13.5), which is 4 rows up. The W frame's bottom edge is row 28 and the player's top edge is row 29. So they're adjacent vertically.
OK so the player can move UP. Now let me try to figure out the game mechanics more carefully.
Looking at the current state:
- Player at (30, 13.5) - rows 29-31, cols 12-15
- W frame at (26, 13.5) - rows 24-28, cols 12-15 (this is the collected G frame)
- c frame at (29.5, 17.5) - rows 28-31, cols 16-19
- Blue bar at (29.5, 33.5) - rows 28-31, cols 28-39
- c frame at (25.5, 45.5) - rows 24-27, cols 44-47
- Pink bar: 33 pixels
The player is now adjacent to the c frame at (29.5, 17.5). The player's right edge is col 15 and the c frame's left edge is col 16. They're horizontally adjacent.
Let me try pressing SPACE now to see what happens when the player is adjacent to a c frame.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at (30, 13.5), adjacent to c frame at (29.5, 17.5)
# Try SPACE
action(['SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
